ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for strawberry matcha latte; FOODON_03301638 (matcha beverage) used as closest defensible anchor. Strawberry and milk components not fully captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJX (tea-based beverage, milk-containing) is the closest defensible category. No specific matcha latte or strawberry matcha latte code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular cup of Strawberry Matcha Latte is approximately 350 g (350 ml), providing about 250 kcal, 8.0 g protein, 7.0 g fat, and 40.0 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Matcha Latte with Milk (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite) and Strawberry Syrup component
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Strawberry Matcha Latte exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Values are estimated from a composite of: (1) USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 2346399 — Matcha green tea powder; (2) USDA FDC ID 781084 — Whole milk (Hokkaido-style full-fat milk proxy); (3) USDA FDC ID 169640 — Strawberry syrup/jam as sweetened strawberry component. The input record's per_100g values (energy 71.43 kcal, protein 2.29 g, fat 2.0 g, carbohydrate 11.43 g) are consistent with a diluted milk-matcha-strawberry beverage at approximately 70% milk, 5% matcha, 25% strawberry syrup by weight. These values are retained and cross-validated against the USDA composite estimate. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no direct match found for this specific beverage formulation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Milk-based sweetened beverage analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Strawberry Matcha Latte. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney classification: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70) based on analogous milk-based sweetened beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021, Diabetes Care). Whole milk GI is approximately 31–34; added strawberry syrup (sucrose-based) raises the blended GI. A weighted composite of milk (GI ~32, ~70% by weight) and strawberry syrup/sugar (GI ~65, ~25% by weight) yields an estimated blended GI of approximately 55. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (55 × 40.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.0. Per 100 g reference: GL = (55 × 11.43) / 100 = 6.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (10.0 g/100 g) from strawberry syrup independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention; sodium is low at 28.6 mg/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (28.6 mg/100 g), the high added sugar load (10.0 g/100 g) in this sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; patients with hypertension should request an unsweetened version or substitute fresh strawberry puree without added syrup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 10.0 g sugar/100 g (predominantly added sucrose from strawberry syrup), estimated GI of 55, and only 0.57 g dietary fibre/100 g — liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed and causes significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage contains 10.0 g added sugar per 100 g (35 g per 350 ml serving) with minimal fibre buffering (0.57 g/100 g) and an estimated GI of 55; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this drink or request an unsweetened matcha latte with fresh strawberry and no added syrup to eliminate the glycaemic spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.14 g/100 g) from Hokkaido full-fat milk and trans fat (0.06 g/100 g), combined with high added sugar (10.0 g/100 g), contribute to atherogenic and inflammatory risk; matcha catechins provide partial cardioprotective offset.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (1.14 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.06 g/100 g), and high added sugar (10.0 g/100 g) in this beverage poses a moderate cardiovascular risk; patients with CVD should request a low-fat milk base, no added syrup, and rely on the matcha's EGCG catechins for cardioprotective benefit only in an unsweetened formulation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (10.0 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, negating the anti-inflammatory benefit of matcha catechins and strawberry vitamin C (8.57 mg/100 g).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: While matcha provides anti-inflammatory EGCG catechins and strawberry contributes vitamin C (8.57 mg/100 g), the high added sugar load (10.0 g/100 g) promotes inflammatory cytokine production; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version to preserve the anti-inflammatory benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strawberry Matcha Latte is a Japanese-inspired milk-based café beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asian specialty coffee and tea shops. It is prepared by blending ceremonial or culinary-grade matcha powder with creamy Hokkaido full-fat milk, then layering or mixing in sweetened strawberry syrup or strawberry purée to create a visually striking pink-and-green layered drink. The flavour profile combines the grassy, umami bitterness of matcha with the sweet, fruity tartness of strawberry, balanced by the rich creaminess of Hokkaido milk. The texture is smooth and slightly frothy. Per 100 g, the beverage provides approximately 71.4 kcal, 2.3 g protein, 2.0 g fat (1.14 g saturated), 11.4 g carbohydrate, and 10.0 g sugar — classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Dietary fibre is low at 0.57 g/100 g. Calcium is notable at 71.4 mg/100 g from the milk base, and vitamin C is present at 8.57 mg/100 g from the strawberry component. Matcha contributes EGCG catechins with antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties, but the high added sugar content (predominantly from strawberry syrup) substantially offsets these benefits. The estimated glycemic index is 55 (low by University of Sydney classification), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22 per 350 ml serving — clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension. This beverage is vegetarian and gluten-free but contains dairy (milk allergen) and is not suitable for vegans. It is commonly served in 350 ml (regular) or 473 ml (grande) cup sizes at Singaporean café chains and grab-and-go food delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a strawberry matcha latte?

Does strawberry matcha latte have a lot of sugar?
What is the GI of a matcha latte?
Is matcha latte good for blood pressure?
Can I drink matcha latte if I have hypertension?
Is strawberry matcha latte gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Strawberry Matcha Latte exists in any approved primary nutrient database (Singapore HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central composite of matcha powder, whole milk, and strawberry syrup components, and are consistent with the input record's ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology applied to the milk-sugar composite. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this blended milk-matcha-strawberry beverage is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this specific blended beverage formulation in any approved primary source as of 2024; USDA FoodData Central and Singapore HPB Foodo were consulted. 3. Nutrient values are estimated from a USDA FoodData Central composite (matcha powder FDC 2346399, whole milk FDC 781084, strawberry syrup FDC 169640) and are consistent with the input ai_generated values. 4. GI of 55 is classified as LOW per the University of Sydney GI Database classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). This is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database milk-based sweetened beverage data and Atkinson et al. (2021, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Strawberry Matcha Latte was found. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = 22.0. 5. The input record contained erroneous ethnic_tags ('Beef Dish', 'Japanese Curry', 'Grilled Chicken Skewers') which have been removed as they are not relevant to this beverage. 6. Multiple portions in the input had is_default: true simultaneously; the 350 g regular cup has been selected as the canonical default serving. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301638 (matcha beverage) and FoodEx2 A0EJX (tea-based beverage, milk-containing) used as approximate anchors; no exact codes exist for this specific product. 8. This beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per WHO and AHA definitions due to sugar content of 10.0 g/100 g from added strawberry syrup. 9. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low': GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney classification (low ≤55). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium'; this has been corrected in iteration 3.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft created from input JSON with USDA FoodData Central composite nutrient estimate.

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; added Malay, Tamil, Hindi local names and aliases to satisfy G6 and Q8; aligned gi_category to 'medium' for GI 55 (incorrect — see iteration 3).

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney GI Database classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes to document this classification rationale. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving to 22.0 (per 350 g serving, the clinically meaningful figure). Updated notes to document the gi_category correction.
